claims.statement: The pS3 repository is licensed under the GNU General Public License v3, not the inherited MIT.
claims.qualification: The LICENSE file at the pinned revision is verbatim GPL-3.0; this is an editorial correction that promotes no runtime behavior.

claims.statement: pS3 self-reports version 0.1.16.
claims.qualification: The shipped binary printed pS3 version 0.1.16 under --version; the inherited row recorded the version as unknown.

claims.statement: The project publishes no releases or tags, so groundwork pinned default-branch HEAD 9428492 as the reviewed revision.
claims.qualification: HEAD is pinned because there is no tag or release to pin to.

claims.statement: The pS3 repository is minimal and dormant: it ships no README, no docs, and no go.mod, and holds 16 commits from a single author, last updated 2024-01-02.
claims.qualification: These repository-shape and staleness facts were recorded from the public repository during groundwork; they bear on provenance, not runtime behavior.

claims.statement: pS3 is the Go command-line tool published at github.com/jboothomas/ps3.
claims.qualification: The repository identity and language were confirmed by an independent clone and source read; no runtime behavior is implied.

claims.statement: The pinned pS3 source does not compile.
claims.qualification: A native arm64 build attempt failed with BUILD_EXIT=1 on missing log and sync/atomic imports, an unused os import, and a selector/type error at cmd/listObjectsV2.go:186; the inherited row called the build straightforward, and the committed prebuilt binary is the only working artifact the project ships.

claims.statement: The pinned pS3 source was confirmed to exist and be publicly obtainable, then independently cloned and fully read.
claims.qualification: The source read is firsthand; every code anchor cites the pinned checkout obtained from the public repository.

claims.statement: pS3 discovers prefixes by a brute-force character-by-character walk rather than S3P-style key bisection, computing no key midpoint anywhere.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the mechanism and the author's blog corroborates it; no runtime listing confirmed it.

claims.statement: When a prefix listing overflows a page, discovery recurses on each extension by computing nextPrefix := currentPrefix + c and launching go discoverPrefixes.
claims.qualification: This is the recursion body at the pinned source; it was not observed in a run.

claims.statement: The overflow branch tests len(Contents) > 999 and never checks IsTruncated, so an exactly-1000-key non-truncated prefix is misclassified as large and needlessly recursed while 1-to-999-key prefixes are emitted directly.
claims.qualification: The inherited threshold-1000 leaf rule is only almost correct because of the missing truncation check.

claims.statement: The discovery alphabet is a fixed 81-element package var (not a compile-time const), is not configurable, and is not every printable byte: it omits several punctuation characters and all non-ASCII bytes.
claims.qualification: The inherited row described a configurable per-printable-byte alphabet; --help exposes no flag for it.

claims.statement: The discoverPrefixes closure is defined at cmd/listObjectsV2.go:196 with its character-loop recursion body at lines 213-241, matching the inherited code anchor.
claims.qualification: The inherited anchor was accurate, which is unusual among the inherited anchors.

claims.statement: Discovery starts from the empty prefix and needs no hints, bootstrap, or prior keyspace knowledge.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the zero-input start; this is the property behind the inherited zero-config strength claim.

claims.statement: The parallel pager fan-out and the printer-worker count are both bounded by a package var maxSemaphore set to 256 and never reassigned.
claims.qualification: The inherited row assumed an unknown but presumably existing concurrency knob.

claims.statement: Prefix-discovery goroutines are launched with no semaphore, so discovery concurrency is unbounded.
claims.qualification: The maxSemaphore bound applies only to the pager, not to go discoverPrefixes.

claims.statement: No command-line flag exposes either concurrency source, so pS3 listing concurrency cannot be capped to the shared CONCURRENCY_CAP of 8.
claims.qualification: Both the 256 pager var and the unbounded discovery goroutines are unexposed; --help shows no concurrency flag.

claims.statement: A --output json-or-text flag exists, but readObjectsV2 ignores it and always prints one fixed line, so the flag is inert in the HEAD source.
claims.qualification: The flag is present in --help but has no effect in the pinned source; the shipped binary may differ.

claims.statement: Whether --output json produces different output in the shipped binary.
claims.qualification: The committed binary diverges from the source and was never run to a listing, so text-versus-json cannot be treated as two real modes.

evidence.reason: The binary was never run to a listing, so its --output behavior is untested and blocked under CREDS=none.

claims.statement: The recursion page-fit threshold and the ListObjectsV2 page size are maxKeys, a package var set to 1000.
claims.qualification: 1000 is S3's own page ceiling, so this is not a pS3-specific deficit.

claims.statement: The full flag and subcommand surface was captured from the binary's --version and --help output.
claims.qualification: The inherited table was flagged as incomplete; the captured help resolves it.

claims.statement: The author's Medium post reports 15,000,000 objects listed in 160 seconds, about 94,000 objects per second, on local non-AWS S3.
claims.qualification: This is a single third-party blog data point (one bucket, one box, one run) and has not been reproduced by this study.

claims.statement: The same post reports pS3 about seven times faster than aws s3api, which it times at 1110 seconds.
claims.qualification: Same single third-party data point; not reproduced.

claims.statement: The same post reports pS3 about five times faster than s5cmd, which it times at 733 seconds, on local S3.
claims.qualification: Same single third-party data point on local non-AWS S3; not reproduced.

claims.statement: Discovery issues speculative per-character LIST calls, up to alphabet-to-the-N calls to resolve an N-character prefix depth.
claims.qualification: Source confirms the speculative LISTs exist; their cost is a separate scale question.

claims.statement: The discovery-LIST overhead at scale, worst on sparse deep-shared-prefix keyspaces, degrades listing cost.
claims.qualification: No benchmark measured API-calls-per-unique-key; the cost at scale is unrun.

evidence.reason: No scale benchmark of the discovery tax has run, blocked under CREDS=none.

claims.statement: Because discovery only extends a prefix with bytes from the fixed 81-character alphabet, any key whose next distinguishing byte is outside that set is never discovered and is silently dropped.
claims.qualification: Source proves the drop by construction; keys under an already-emitted small prefix are safe. This is also the qualifier on the inherited arbitrary-keyspace strength claim. The inherited framing that such out-of-alphabet keys are rare in practice was only an assertion, not established.

claims.statement: Because discovery is a byte-by-byte prefix walk that needs no delimiter hierarchy, pS3 does not degenerate on flat buckets the way delimiter-based listing does, so it works on arbitrary keyspaces in principle.
claims.qualification: This is a source-corroborated structural property; it is bounded by the fixed 81-character alphabet, so out-of-alphabet keys are still silently dropped (claim out-of-alphabet-keys-dropped).

claims.statement: A live listing silently drops out-of-alphabet keys on a non-ASCII or arbitrary-byte bucket.
claims.qualification: Demonstration needs an edge fixture (EDGE_BUCKET=none, deferred) and credentials, which were blocked.

evidence.reason: No edge-key bucket was configured and no credentialed run was possible.

claims.statement: The headline throughput and speedup multipliers reproduce off the author's box.
claims.qualification: Blocked: no benchmark has run; this is the benchmark phase's job.

evidence.reason: No comparative benchmark has run, blocked under CREDS=none.

claims.statement: The published speedup multipliers can be checked internally because aws-cli and s5cmd are themselves study subjects.
claims.qualification: Correct in principle and deferred to the benchmark phase; nothing is promoted now.

evidence.reason: No comparative benchmark against the study's own aws-cli and s5cmd baselines has run.

claims.statement: The shipped binary's build metadata records Go 1.20.3 with aws-sdk-go v1.44.249, cobra v1.7.0, and viper v1.15.0.
claims.qualification: These versions were read from the committed binary's build metadata and recorded in the build receipt.

claims.statement: The only working artifact is upstream's committed prebuilt binary, which is amd64; because the source does not compile there is no native arm64 build, so every probe that ran the binary ran the amd64 image under qemu emulation on the arm64 runner.
claims.qualification: Emulation was acceptable for the smoke probes but is disqualifying for benchmark timing, which needs one natively-common architecture; the source-compile attempt itself ran natively on arm64.

claims.statement: pS3 builds its AWS session with the default shared-config credential chain only, with no --no-sign-request flag and no anonymous-credentials path.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the absence and a no-credentials trace shows NoCredentialProviders; the general no-unsigned-path claim is source-and-observation supported, not settled by the single committed receipt.

claims.statement: Under the harness anonymous environment, the single list-objects-v2 --bucket noaa-normals-pds --region us-east-1 invocation of pS3 v0.1.16 exited 1 and produced no listing.
claims.qualification: The committed receipt settles exactly this one invocation, which failed at S3 session creation. The general no-unsigned-path conclusion and the 5-of-5 non-trace plus 3-of-3 trace determinism are separate observations, not this receipt.

claims.statement: The list-object-versions and head-objects modes were not run and are blocked by the same shared session path.
claims.qualification: Their blocked state is inferred from the shared session code path, not from their own receipts.

evidence.reason: Neither mode was run; the block is inferred from the shared session path.

claims.statement: With a bare no-credentials environment (only AWS_EC2_METADATA_DISABLED=true, no config or credentials-file redirect), pS3 exits 0 with zero objects and no error, a false success indistinguishable from an empty bucket.
claims.qualification: This is an observation, not a committed wrapper receipt; the harness's stricter starvation instead fails at session creation with exit 1, and only that exit-1 half is receipt-backed.

claims.statement: The repository at HEAD cannot reproduce its own shipped binary: pS3.0-1-16 exposes head-objects, list-object-versions, and list-test subcommands whose source files are absent from the checkout, and the present source does not compile.
claims.qualification: The file-set mismatch and compile failure are receipt-backed; the inference that the binary came from a newer or never-pushed tree is not.

claims.statement: On the list-objects-v2 path, once an AWS session is created, pS3 first calls GetBucketLocation, and its fallback sets the region to empty when --region us-east-1 is passed and to us-west-1 when no region is passed for a us-east-1 bucket.
claims.qualification: The region-selection bug is source-level; the harness anonymous run failed at session creation before reaching GetBucketLocation, so its receipt does not exercise the fallback, while a bare-no-credentials observation traced the fallback firing to an empty region.

claims.statement: The discovery helper s3ListObjectsWithBackOff returns its response with a nil error on any failure, making the backoff below it dead code and handing callers a nil or partial response they then dereference.
claims.qualification: This error-swallowing nil-dereference risk was not triggered at smoke because the run died earlier at session creation.

claims.statement: The --debug and --trace flags suppress object output and print only a count, so listing objects and request-level logging cannot be obtained together.
claims.qualification: Source-level behavior in readObjectsV2; not exercised in a listing run.

claims.statement: pS3 streams objects from the producer through an unbuffered channel to a printer pool rather than building a full in-memory slice of all keys; the discovered prefix list, bounded by --prefix-count, is the only such accumulation.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the streaming channel mechanics only; whether this bounds object memory at scale is an unverified hypothesis carried by claim memory-throughput-at-scale, and no scale run measured memory.

claims.statement: Memory and throughput with the in-memory prefix list and 256 printer workers at millions of keys.
claims.qualification: The streaming model suggests bounded object memory, but scale behavior is untested and blocked.

evidence.reason: No scale run was possible, blocked under CREDS=none.

claims.statement: The --prefix-count flag, default 500, is the primary discovery cutoff: once the large-prefix target is reached, further descent stops and prefixes are handed to the parallel pager.
claims.qualification: Source establishes the cutoff and default; the knob was not swept.
